turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Jackson has the opinions of the Court in case 23-1002, Hewitt versus United States and 23-1150, Duffey versus United States.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Section 924(c) of Title 18 of the United States Code criminalizes possessing a firearm while committing other crimes. Before the First Step Act was enacted in 2018, federal judges were required to sentence Section 924(c) offenders to stacked 25-year periods of imprisonment for each second or subsequent Section 924(c) conviction, even if those convictions were part of the same criminal offense. As a practical matter, this meant that courts had to impose sentences that lasted decades or even centuries on many first-time Section 924(c) offenders. Congress eliminated this harsh mandatory minimum penalty in Section 403(a) of the First Step Act of 2018. And importantly for present purposes, in Section 403(b) of that act, Congress made Section 403(a)'s more lenient penalty partially retroactive, meaning available to some, but not all offenders who had committed Section 924(c) offenses prior to the passage of the act. Section 403(b) states "this section shall apply to any offense that was committed before the date of enactment of this act if a sentence for the offense has not been imposed as of such date of enactment." The question presented today concerns an edge case. Which penalties apply to a Section 924(c) offender who had been sentenced for their offense before the First Step Act was enacted? But that sentence was subsequently vacated such that the offender must now face a post-act resentencing. We hold that in such a circumstance, a sentence has not been imposed for purposes of Section 403(b), and therefore the First Step Act’s more lenient penalties apply. To reach that conclusion, we look first to the words that Congress chose in Section 403(b). Notably, the operative phrase was not written in the past tense or past perfect tense, excluding anyone upon whom a sentence had been imposed. Rather, Congress employed the present perfect tense. So at the time of resentencing, judges must ask whether a sentence has been imposed on the defendant, and the present perfect tense requires some relevant connection to, well, the present. The act in question is entirely a relic of the past. It does not follow naturally from Congress’ use of the present perfect tense. To understand this distinction, consider a hypothetical. Suppose the US Olympic Committee enacted a rule stating that athletes may call themselves Olympic champions if a gold medal has been awarded to them. Under that rule, a US sprinter who took first place a decade ago in the Olympic 100-meter finals could validly proclaim today that she is an Olympic champion. But if that sprinter was stripped of her medal in the intervening years because she used performing enhancing drugs, she can no longer call herself an Olympic champion under the rule today. She had been awarded an Olympic medal, but that action was entirely undone. The same concept applies here. Petitioners Hewitt, Duffey, and Ross were sentenced for multiple Section 924(c) convictions under the harsh pre-First Step Act 25-year stacked sentencing scheme. But their sentences were vacated after the passage of the act, so they had to be re-sentenced. Petitioners argued at their resentencing that the act’s lessened mandatory minimums should govern, and today, we agree. Employing essentially the same analysis that generated the outcome in my sprinter hypothetical, that is, while petitioners had been sentenced prior to the enactment of the First Step Act by the time of their resentencing, those initial sentences had been vacated and were relics of the past. Thus, the sentencing judge should have determined that a sentence has not been imposed on them for purposes of Section 403(b) and as a result, should have applied the First Step Act’s lessened mandatory minimums. Our conclusion is also supported by the nature of vacatur. When interpreting statutes, we recognize that congress legislates against the backdrop of certain unexpressed presumptions, and one such presumption is that vacated court orders or void ab initio and thus lack any prospective legal effect. By operation of legal fiction, the law acts as though the vacated order, conviction, or sentence never occurred. That principle applies here as well. A vacated sentence does not operate to prevent a defendant who is being resentenced from receiving the First Step Act’s penalties. Instead, only extant sentences with continuing validity preclude application of the First Step Act. For those reasons and others we lay out in our opinion, we reverse and remand the judgment of the Fifth Circuit. Chief Justice Roberts and Justice Gorsuch join all but parts four and five of the Court’s opinion. Justice Alito filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Thomas, Kavanaugh, and Barrett join.
